sentences.text: Yes.
It was always my dream that someday I would take my children back to my hometown and to the house where I was raised and to show them their roots.
And it became a reality in 1979.
I think it was-- or was it-- No.
It was 1975.
I'm sorry. 1975.
I think both children were still in college.
Joani was two years ahead of Joel.
And as a matter of fact, it was arranged so that the two of them would have a taste of Europe by themselves by going to England for a week together.
And we then met up with them in Germany.
Meanwhile.
My husband has two-- had two brothers.
And each brother had one of his children along.
And we all met eventually in their hometown of Andernach.
We stayed in a small hotel right on the Rhine embankment.
And we kind of took over a whole floor of the hotel.
Also, my-- my nanny joined us there with her husband.
And we visited the home where my husband was born and raised.
And that house, by the way, had a date on it from the 1600s.
It was a very old house.
And Andernach was a very old town.
Pardon?
It's near Koblenz.
It's on the Rhine.
It was founded by the Romans.
And as a matter of fact, they had their thousandth anniversary a few years ago, the town of Andernach.
And there were Jews there already I think in the 10th century.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] After-- after meeting in Andernach, I took my children to Rheydt.
But this time, I didn't do what I did the last time.
I had written to the owner of the house.
And I explained to her that I was coming to Germany with my children, I would very much appreciate if she would allow me one more time to show my children the house where I was born and raised.

And she wrote back to me that by now she wasn't living there herself anymore, that she had converted the house into two apartments, but that she would instruct her tenants to allow us to come in and see the house.
So when we came to Rheydt-- by the way, in Andernach, we also went to the cemeteries.
The Jewish cemetery in Andernach was beautifully kept by the-- by the town.
And the children could see the Loeb family burial grounds and-- and have a reality of their heritage and of their roots.
We did the same thing in-- in Rheydt.
Actually, my mother's family was buried in the next little town, Odenkirchen, which is where my-- my mother was born.
That cemetery was not in such in a great condition, unfortunately.
But we were able, again, to show them the family names, Salmon family and Simon family, other family names that were of the parents and grandparents and great-grandparents and so on.
My mother's family was in that town for several generations, whereas my father had moved into the area from southern Germany.
And I took the children into the house.
And we took some pictures.
The house had undergone some changes inside, decorating and architecture, since it was now a two-family house.
It is in beautiful condition.
It is a house of a particular architectural style and they really put it into pristine condition.
Also, the garden was, again, very nice.
We had a big garden at one time.
But I think a bomb had killed every plant and every fruit tree during the war.
And at that time, it started to look a little bit like it was before.
And after we-- after we left the house, my children said, you know, Mom, it was like we were visiting Opa Zep.
Opa Zep was my father.
His nickname was Zep.
And I always talked a great deal about him to my children, whatever came up, you know, because I have many fond memories of things that we did together or things that we talked about.
And my children were-- it was as though they knew him.
And they felt they had had a visit with him by visiting the house.
And that gave me such a warm and good feeling.
Another thing that happened on that trip-- and I remember exactly where it was.
We were on a small funicular going up some mountain along the Rhine.
And the kids-- and we had not really talked about how we felt about going back to Germany.
We didn't want them to have any preconceived notions, actually.
And the kids said to me, you know, Mom, it's nice to meet some of the young people we're meeting.
But we always feel a little funny when we meet people of Omi's age.
Omi was my mother.
That's-- that's what they called her.
And you know, that was a revelation to me that they got this feeling, because naturally, those were the people who were our persecutors, my mother's generation.
We were children.
But our parents were the ones who were persecuted by their peers.
And somehow, the children got this feeling.
And I just felt this was a complete success, this trip.
It was very pleasant to be there with their cousins, you know.
And my husband took them to all the places that he cherished as a youngster.
We went on top of a mountain near Andernach where he used to go with his friends, and-- and places where he wrote his bike, and to small towns along the Rhine that are still very picturesque.
And we'd stop in restaurants and-- and enjoy the view of the boats going up and down the river and so on.
It was a most pleasant experience.
Yes.
My oldest brother-in-law was Dr.
Ernst Loeb.
He was a professor at the Queen's College in Kingston, Ontario, professor of Germanics.
His wife Margot was not along on that trip, but his daughter Karen was.
She was actually married to a German boy and living at the time near Frankfurt.
Karen today lives in the Seattle area, where her brother Dennis also lives, so part of the Loeb family lives in Seattle.
My younger brother, Kurt Loeb, was along with his wife Lucille, who is-- lives five minutes away from here and we're very good friends.
And he had his son Mark along.
Mark has a brother Bart.
And the two of them are in business together and they live in Ann Arbor, Michigan.
My brother-in-law now lives in-- in Sarasota.
He's divorced from Lucille.
But we see him in the wintertime and I see Lucille often.
My next trip to Germany was in 1982, when the city of Bonn invited me, since I had been living there for a short time.
We had heard about these get-togethers.
And the city of Bonn paid for me and for my husband the fare.
When we arrived there, our hotel, arrangements had been made in one of the first-class hotels, the Konigshof on the Rhine.
And we also got 200 marks of spending money.

The whole week had been programmed with not only entertainment, such as trips, boat trips on the Rhine and a concert for the Beethoven festival that was there and a wonderful party by the chancellor, a spring festival at the chancellor's house, we were also invited to attend the government institutions.

The reason for the invitations that are still happening and were at that time in full swing was to extend a hand of reconciliation, to build a bridge with their expatriates that are still alive, and to have some communication with them.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And these invitations were made at the same time with feelings of trepidation by the hosts as to how they would be received, these invitations, and how we would react to them when we come there.

As it turns out, the visits all turned out very well, especially in the city of Bonn, first of all because they seemed to have the financial means and the facilities to organize a week of programs, as I said not just entertainment, but also for instance conversations with young people and inter-religious conversations between Jews and non-Jews.
We were hosted by private families on occasion.
And we had much contact with interested citizens.
Naturally, there were also disinterested citizens for these visits.
And this happened all over.
Almost all the major cities in Germany and many small towns have organized these visits.
It happens that the city of Bonn is still doing it.
There are many people who-- many of the people surviving live in Europe and want to meet each other in their hometown every year.
So this time, this-- each year, the city invites anybody who's ever been there and anybody who hasn't been there yet.
The people who've been there before come at their own expense, both transportation-wise and hotel-wise.
But the city organizes a week full of programs that they provide.
I don't think any other city does that but Bonn.
And each year I get an invitation, but I haven't gone back since.
I have a friend who does go back once in a while, who-- the daughter of the cantor whom I mentioned before.
She has gone back once or twice.
That was in 1982.
And in 1989, I received an invitation from my hometown of Rheydt.
And I was invited to bring an accompanist, husbands.
And in my children-- in my case, it was one of my children.
My mother received an invitation and she was very loath to go.
She really never wanted to go back to Germany.
And I convinced her she must go because she's the one who remembers the most.
She's of the generation that knows so much, so much more, because we were children.
And I convinced her, and so the two of us took both Joani and Joel along.
Again, the-- our fare was provided.
Our hotel arrangements were provided.
We did not get additional spending money, but that wasn't important anyway.
But what I was so excited about this time was that I was going to meet some of my school friends, whom I haven't seen since we were 10 years old.
Let me tell you, that's a culture shock.
You remember this little boy, this tow-haired little kid that you grew up with.
In this case, it was my father's partner's son.
And you know, being an only child, I was so much at their house, 'cause they had four children and each child had friends over all the time.
And I loved being over there.
And we were together almost every day.
So we-- we traveled.
And first of all, we saw people we knew in the hotel already.
And the first event was a get-- a get-together to meet each other in the town hall in Monchengladbach.
This time Rheydt is no longer a separate community, but Monchengladbach has three towns combined into one municipality now, the town where my mother was, Odenkirchen, Rheydt, and Monchengladbach.
And the-- some of the assemblies took place in Monchengladbach and some of them took place in Rheydt.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We also had a team along from the television show, because somebody was related to somebody who was connected with that show.
And they filmed the whole thing.
My daughter also filmed the whole thing.
And I think her-- her film is just as good as the other one.
And there we were in a big hall with some familiar faces, with some faintly familiar faces.
And somebody pointed out a portly man to me with glasses and gray hair and a mustache and he wore a hat.
And they said, there he is-- his sisters to be exact.
And there was this little tow-haired kid 50 years later.
Culture shock.
Not only that, I fell around his neck and kissed him and hugged him and he looked at me like, who's this crazy woman?
He-- he's very orthodox and this was strictly taboo.
What is his name, Liesl?
I'd rather not say.
He lives in Brazil.
And he didn't see fit to communicate with me whatsoever.
But I-- I do see one of his sisters every year when I go to Israel.
She lives in Israel.
And we commiserate about old times.
As I said, her father was my father's partner.
And so it's-- it's a touch of home for both of us when we get together.
There's another friend who lives in Atlanta.
Her name is Erica Hecht.
And her father-- her father or her uncle was the controller in my grandfather's factory.
We were-- we were in the same school, but she's not of the same age.
But there also is a touch of home.
You know, even though we were thrown out-- we were thrown out of our homes.
We were kicked out.
But there is this-- there is this feeling for home and roots that doesn't leave you no matter what.
And some people can't understand that.
They can't understand why we would want to go back to Germany, why we would want to be the guests of the nation that didn't want us in the first place.
They don't understand.
First of all, because we wanted to see each other again, see who's coming.
And secondly, because that's where our roots are.
One those cemeteries are buried our grandparents and our great-grandparents.
And our communities were built up by some of our contributions and our family's contributions.
And most of all, we have the chance to talk to the young people there.
And that was one of my big reasons for going.
When the invitation arrived, first of all, we got a letter from a family who said they were going to be our hosts.
It was husband and wife, each one a teacher, each one born in the '40s, so they were not of the Hitler generation.
They had two daughters.
And they invited us to their home and they came to see us.
And there were flowers in our rooms from them.
And they are the loveliest people.
I'm still in touch with them.
They were very gracious to us and joined the program.
This was voluntary.
There were people there that didn't come out of the walls while we were there, you know.
But it was important.
I spoke in several schools, in a-- in a Catholic school, an elementary school, and in a high school.
Many of the children had been prepared by reading literature about the Holocaust or leading-- reading stories.
They asked intelligent questions.
And they were very concerned whether we blame them.
That was a universal question, do you blame us?
We can't tell young people that we blame them, you know.
I think they felt relieved.

And since this was the one and only invitation that our town extended, simply because they just invited everybody who survived, because it was a small Jewish community in the first place, they too had their trepidations about how we would receive them and how they would receive us, and whether there would be animosity or whatever.
And it doesn't happen.
It really doesn't happen.

I've heard from so many people who've gone over with a chip on their shoulder or who go over who say, well, we'll take what we can get from them, but we don't really want to go, or as somebody expressed in our group, my heart said yes and my brain said no, you know.
And you come away feeling better.
You definitely come away feeling better.
And I think these visits are important, because some of these young people don't know, what is a Jew?
They have no-- a clue, you know.
Are we people with horns?
Are we murderers with blood on our hands of Christian babies, or who the heck knows what they think?
And what was the reason for your trip to Miami?
The trip to Miami took place the same year, 1989, which was the 50th anniversary from 1939 of the voyage of the One of the survivors was a man by the name of Herbert Karliner, who lives in Miami.
Herbert Karliner was 13-year-old boy on the when it passed the beachfront of Miami.
You know, the captain had tried to land us in Miami.
He came very close and the American government sent military planes to make sure that we would keep moving, and the-- and the shore patrol.
The Congress sent those planes down to make sure we kept on moving.
We were that close.
We could see the hotels on the beach.
We were that close.
And he thought to himself, if I ever have the chance, that's where I want to live.
Herbert Karliner ended up in France.
He was-- his parents took him and his brother to an orphanage.
He had two sisters.
And the two sisters and his parents did not survive.
The boys who were hidden in an orphanage did.
He came to the States after the war and he went to Miami to live.
He opened a bakery.
He did well for himself.
And he eventually wanted to give back to the orphanage something.
And so he wrote to them that he would like to support a child in that orphanage.
And after a while, he started getting letters from a teenage girl.
To make a long story short, after three years he went over there and he married her.
He lives in Miami now.
He's retired.
He's very active with the Miami Holocaust Memorial.
And he was the one who organized a reunion of survivors on the occasion of the 50th anniversary of our passing Miami Beach.
He had the help of a local politician, also a survivor, actually.
I can't think of his name right now.
And they-- they got in touch with-- I don't know how they found various people.
But we were 29 actual survivors in Miami, plus entourage, husbands, wives, friends, and whatever.
And we were lodged at the Fontainebleau hotel.

I was rooming with a young woman that I had met in the interim through my appearance on with my mother when the film came out She lives in Boston and we're still very much in touch, and we were roommates together.
We arrive at the Fontainebleau and I want to check in.
And the person says, Mr.
So-and-so paid for your room.
I thought to myself, what do they think of me, Mr.
So-and-so is paying for my room?
But apparently, he had-- this politician had decided to-- to do that.
You know, he footed the bill for our hotel stay in Miami.
And this was actually a weekend occasion.
We had, again, a meeting, a get-together.
And most of us didn't recognize each other.
We were children on board.
There was-- the oldest person who came was a 90-some-year-old lady, who was remarkable in her presence of mind.
And she was, of course, of my mother's generation.
My mother didn't make the trip.
She didn't feel up to it.
She was still alive at that time, but she didn't feel up to it.
The youngest was a 51-year-old very beautiful woman who was a baby on the arms of her parents on the ship.
And somebody had brought photographs and we were looking at photographs from the ship.
And she said, oh, that's me and my parents.
She saw herself on the arms of her parents.
They had ended up in France.
When the Germans invaded, her parents had given her to a farmer, had informed relatives here in this country that they have given the child to this farmer.
And the parents perished.
After the war, the uncle and aunt came to France and found her and took her back to the States and raised her.
I can't.
I don't remember.
I'm sorry.
But it was one of the outstanding things of our visit to Miami.
One of the unpleasant things that happened was that this politician somehow had found what was supposed to be Adolf Hitler's yacht was a wreck.
And-- and they wanted to blow it up in front of us.
And we really did not like this particular part of our visit.
And then he also arranged for a private yacht to take us to the spot where the passed out to sea a little bit.
And it was just as I remembered how close we were to the beach and to Collins Avenue and to the hotels.
Of course, now there are many more than there were then.
But it was such a friendly scene that we saw as we passed by and were followed by the military plane so many years ago.
We had on board some of this politician's supporters as well.
But otherwise, just the survivors.
None of their accompanying people were on board.
We had-- we had a rabbi and a-- and a priest and a minister on board as well who all said prayers.
And we threw flowers into the water for the people who didn't survive.
And then afterwards, there were many parties and so on.
We got the key to the city and all that sort of thing.
And we came away with good feelings.
But-- but to my regret, we haven't been able to stay in touch much with each other.
The next contact with people was the one that was in Skokie, Illinois to make a documentary, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the one that I showed you the tape to.
I can't think of the name right now.
It's-- it's over there.
It was nine of us got together.
And some of us have been together in Miami.
We made this documentary.
And then one more time a documentary was made in 1993.
And that was made by a Canadian film company for a television presentation.
The producer was an Iranian Christian man-- the director, rather.
The producer was a French Jew.
The camera man was Dutch.
It was a very international crew.
And there were I think again nine of us who were together.
Some of us had met before in Miami or in Illinois.
Some of us hadn't met before.
And we did a film on board a cruise ship to get the feeling of being on high seas.
And that film is called and as a matter of fact has been shown in France, in Finland, in Canada, in Britain and Ireland.
It will be shown here on August 29, 1996, on the Discovery Channel.
And that experience was a very interesting experience as well.
At that time, my daughter Joan accompanied me at the invitation of the director.

I think that everything really goes back to a few things, the-- the choices and the decisions of my parents as to our emigration, the kindness and generosity of the Blum family to bring us to this country, and I have good fortune of getting away with our lives by the skin of our teeth, and I thank God for all of that.
